reasoning: The effective yield on the ICE BofA US Corporate B Index is currently hovering around 6.92%, having drifted downwards from mid-2024. However, fundamental indicators suggest that bond yields face asymmetric upside risk: high-yield credit spreads are near historic tights (around 300 bps), and inflation stickiness has caused markets to price out most Federal Reserve rate cuts for 2025 (CME FedWatch indicates a near-zero chance of a March cut, and rising expectations of 'no cuts' through 2025). This means underlying Treasury rates likely remain structurally high or face upward pressure while tight corporate spreads leave significant room for yields to jump if economic conditions weaken or bankruptcies tick up. On a very short-term horizon, recent momentum and seasonal analysis provided by FRED suggest a slightly elevated probability of the yield increasing. Following system instructions to strictly increase uncertainty across longer horizons (forcing probabilities closer to 0.5 for distant dates), I scale my near-term fundamental estimate of modest probability of an increase linearly towards a 1-year estimate effectively at a coin-flip.
